trajectory_summary: The call ran clean structurally — verify beats hit in order, credit qualifier delivered correctly — but the rep never built any energy. The customer's prior skepticism about prices and terms was acknowledged twice and dropped twice, leaving no emotional hook to carry the call forward. When the credit DQ landed, there was nothing to fall back on.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ended as a DQ on stated credit with a soft follow-up promise that was immediately contradicted. No appointment was booked. Outcome should be tagged: disqualified_stated_credit / no_appointment.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Emotional anchor depth on prior-experience signal
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I looked into the whole thing before, but I didn't like it because of the prices, because of the terms, and a lot of stuff.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The customer handed the rep the most valuable signal in the call — a qualified re-engagement from someone who had already researched solar, formed specific objections, and waited for the market to mature. The rep acknowledged it twice and dropped it twice. This is the verify.interest_in_solar depth gap the schema identifies as the difference between top and bottom setters. On this call, building on that signal was the only path to creating enough curiosity to survive the credit DQ conversation. The rep needs to practice sitting with this kind of disclosure for 20–30 seconds and converting it into a forward-looking hook.

key_moments.quote: I looked into the whole thing before, but I didn't like it because of the prices, because of the terms, and a lot of stuff.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a qualified re-engagement signal, not just an objection. A customer who looked into solar before, formed specific opinions about prices and terms, waited for the market to mature, and is now back on the phone is a warmer lead than a cold inbound. The rep heard 'prior dissatisfaction' and moved on. The strategic read is that this was the emotional anchor the entire call should have been built around — 'you waited for things to get better, let's see if they did for your home specifically.'

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Yeah. Kinda get a little bit more research through. Yeah. Is this, how long ago was the last time you looked into it?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second pass at the customer's prior-bad-experience signal, and the rep again acknowledged without engaging. The rep's pivot to a timeline question ('how long ago') is a verify-checklist move — it captures data but does not build emotional alignment. The schema's verify.interest_in_solar directive calls for depth and emotional anchoring here. The rep's line 'Times have definitely changed' at 01:17 was the closest thing to a curiosity hook in the call, but it was delivered as a throwaway rather than a genuine pivot. Two touches on the same concern, zero depth on either.

key_moments.quote: No. It's not that much. No. It's bad.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer self-reports a credit score below 670. The schema's qualifiers.credit_qualifier section explicitly flags this as an open policy question — setter-level DQ on stated credit may be killing real deals on unreliable self-reporting. The rep accepted the stated number without any exploration. The strategic read would be that the rep is following the script correctly. The reactive read is that the rep is DQ'ing on a number the customer may be misreporting (Vantage vs FICO, bureau variation, etc.). The schema does not resolve this — it flags it as a question for Kenny. The coaching gap here is not that the rep DQ'd; it's that the rep had no tool to handle the ambiguity.

key_moments.quote: Is that the the same thing with your wife as well? Is it below 670? Yeah. The same thing.
key_moments.stewart_read: The wife was identified as a co-owner at 01:43 ('Only me and my wife'). The rep correctly noted her presence in the verify section. At 02:25, the rep checks the wife's credit as a secondary qualifier — which is procedurally correct. However, the wife was never engaged as a co-decision-maker in the appointment-setting sense. The schema's protocols.spouse_decision directive requires that a spouse on title be included in the appointment. In this call, the credit DQ made that moot — but the pattern is worth noting: the wife appeared in the verify section as a data point, not as a person whose presence at the appointment needed to be secured.
